title: practical english usage - Part 197
content:
May can be used to suggest ignorance or uncertainty.

5  whoever,         etc   ... may I stay with Monica whenever I go to London.

Whenever I see you I feel nervous.

Whenever can suggest repetition, in the sense of 'every time that’ .

4 whenever           =  'every    time    that' grammar •  253  no matter who, etc For sentences like Something's the matter with my foot, ►    524.

I'll always love you, no matter what. (= ... no matter what happens.) No matter what can be used at the end of a clause, without a following verb.

4 no   matter        what It doesn't matter when you come.

To  introduce just one clause, we  can use It doesn't matter.

No matter when you come, you'll be welcome, (          No matter-when two clauses.

Because no matter who, etc act like conjunctions, they must be used with 3   no   matter        and   it  doesn't       matter you like.) but not   oh You can have whichever you like, (           Y  can have no matter which WILT* rrCV"J but not Whatever you do is fine with me. (          No matter-what you do is fine objects. Clauses with no matter who, etc cannot be used in this way.

However, clauses with whoever/whatever/whichever can be used as subjects or Call me when you arrive, however late it is.

- Call me when you arrive, no matter how late it is.

Whatever you say, I won't believe you.

- No matter what you say, I won't believe you.

whatever, etc (►  252). Compare: The  expressions no matter who/what, etc are used rather like whoever, 2  no    matter        who,     etc   and   whoever,         etc You'll be welcome no matter when you come.

No matter where you go, you'll find people who love music.

We   use a present tense with a future meaning after no matter (►  231).

No matter where you go, I'll follow you.

I'll love you no matter what you do.

The  meaning is similar to 'it is not important who/what, etc’ .

These expressions are similar to conjunctions, and introduce adverbial clauses.

No matter can be used with who, whose, what, which, where, when and how.

253 no   matter        who,     etc Other Adverbial ClausesSection 23 grammar •  254  whether... or.. .

as was agreed; as is well known.

Common expressions with as in place of a subject: as follows; as was expected; not We've got food for as many people as want it. (     ... as-they-want it-.) yesterday.) not The train might be late, as happened yesterday, (     .. . as it happened not (     . . . nnne than they canw last y^ftr.) There were a lot of people at the exhibition - more than came last year.

He worries more than is necessary, (     .. .-mere-than -it/whatis necessary.) pronouns).

Than and as can take the place of subjects in clauses (rather like relative 1 subjects:     more      than     is  necessary;          as   happened subjects,      etc           _ 256 than-     and   as-dauses:      leaving      out For the word order in structures like I did as good a job as I could, ►     187.

As cold as it was, we went out.

In American English, as ... as is common.

Tired as she was, I decided not to disturb her. (= Because she was so tired ...) Occasionally, the structure with as can be used to give a reason.

Scot though she was, she supported the English team.

After a noun, though can be used in this way (but not as).

Strange as/though it may seem, I don 't like watching cricket.

Much  as/though I respect your point of view, I can't agree.

Bravely as/though they fought, they had no chance of winning.

Cold as/though it was, we went out. (= Although it was very cold,...) (In American English only as is normally used like this; though is unusual.) In this case they both mean ’ although’ , and suggest an emphatic contrast.

As and though can be used in a special structure after an adjective or adverb.

adjective/adverb/noun               + as   + clause 255  as   and   thou     gh:   s pecial     word    or der For whether and if, ►    261.

Whether you like it or whether you don't,...

Whether you like it or not,...  Whether or not you like it,...

Several structures are possible with whether... or not.
